Step 0: Safely Power Off and Remove the SD Card
Step 1: Identify the SD Card Device
Option A (Recommended): Create a Compressed Image (.img.gz)
Option B: Create an Uncompressed Image (.img)
Restore the Backup Image to an SD Card
Can I back up an SD card while it’s still running in the device?
What if I restore to a bigger SD card?
What if I restore to a smaller SD card?

frontMatter.title: How to Back Up an SD Card (Full OS Image) to an IMG File Using Linux Command Line
frontMatter.author: Amrut Prabhu
frontMatter.summary: Create a complete SD card backup image (.img or .img.gz) on Linux using command line tools. Restore the image anytime to recover an OS and all installed software if the card gets corrupted.

CPU.Cores:
4 × Cortex-A720 up to 2.6GHz
4 × Cortex-A720 up to 2.4GHz
4 × Cortex-A520 up to 1.8GHz

GPU.Support:
Vulkan 1.4
OpenGL ES 4.3
OpenCL 3.0
Hardware ray tracing

specifications.Video Output:
1 × DisplayPort 1.4 (up to 4K@120Hz, MST supported)
1 × HDMI 2.0 (up to 4K@60Hz)
1 × USB-C (USB 3.2 Gen2 + DisplayPort Alt Mode, up to 4K@60Hz)
specifications.USB:
1 × USB-C (USB 3.2 Gen2 10Gbps + DP Alt Mode)
2 × USB-A (USB 3.2 Gen2 10Gbps)
3 × USB-A (USB 2.0)
specifications.Power:
12V DC via 5525 Barrel Jack
12V DC via 4-pin Power Connector.
specifications.Audio: No dedicated 3.5mm audio jack (audio via HDMI/DP)
specifications.Cooling:
4-pin CPU fan header with PWM control + TACH feedback
75×75mm heatsink mounting holes
specifications.Other Expansion Capabilities:
1 × M.2 E-Key (PCIe Gen4 x2 + USB) for wireless modules
1 × M.2 B-Key (USB) for cellular modules
1 x Nano SIM slot
40-pin GPIO header
